---
title: Fourth Tutorial for R4DS
author: David Kane
tutorial:
  id: r4ds-4
output:
  learnr::tutorial:
    df_print: default
    progressive: true
    allow_skip: true
runtime: shiny_prerendered
description: 'This tutorial for "R for Data Science" covers Chapter 16: Factors, Chapter 17: Dates and Times, Chapter 18: Missing Values, Chapter 19: Joins, and Chapter 22: Arrow. Exercises use NBA and FIFA datasets to practice forcats, lubridate, table joins, and reading parquet files with arrow.'
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(learnr)
library(tutorial.helpers)

library(tidyverse)
library(arrow)
library(plotly)
library(viridis)
library(scales)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(out.width = '90%')
options(tutorial.exercise.timelimit = 60,
        tutorial.storage = "local")

nba_clean <- open_dataset("data/game.parquet") |>
  collect() |>
  left_join(
    open_dataset("data/line_score.parquet") |> collect(),
    by = "game_id",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    game_date = ymd_hms(game_date),
    game_date_est = ymd_hms(game_date_est)
  ) |>
  drop_na(pts_home.x) |>
  mutate(
    game_date = as_datetime(game_date),
    game_year = year(game_date),
    season_label = paste0(season_id %/% 10000, "-", str_sub(season_id + 1, 3, 4))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(game_date), game_year >= 1946) |>
  mutate(
    wl_home = factor(wl_home, levels = c("W", "L")),
    game_era = case_when(
      game_year <= 1979 ~ "Pre-Three Point",
      game_year <= 1999 ~ "Modern Era",
      game_year <= 2019 ~ "Analytics Era",
      TRUE ~ "Current Era"
    ),
    game_era = factor(game_era, levels = c("Pre-Three Point", "Modern Era", "Analytics Era", "Current Era"))
  ) |>
  mutate(
    across(contains("pct"), ~replace_na(.x, 0)),
    across(c(pts_home.x, pts_away.x), ~replace_na(.x, 0)),
    total_points = pts_home.x + pts_away.x
  ) |>
  filter(total_points > 0, !is.na(pts_home.x), !is.na(pts_away.x)) |>
  group_by(game_year, game_era, season_label) |>
  summarise(
    avg_points = mean(total_points, na.rm = TRUE),
    games_count = n(),
    home_win_pct = mean(wl_home == "W", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(game_year)

final_fifa <- "data/fifa.parquet" |>
  open_dataset() |>
  collect() |>
  mutate(
    height_cm = as.numeric(str_extract(Height, "\\d+")),
    weight_kg = as.numeric(str_extract(Weight, "\\d+"))
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~str_replace_all(.x, "\\n", ""))) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~str_trim(.x))) |>
  mutate(
    joined_date = mdy(Joined),
    years_at_club = as.numeric(today() - joined_date) / 365.25,
    long_tenure = years_at_club > 10
  ) |>
  drop_na(joined_date) |>
  mutate(
    value_numeric = case_when(
      is.na(Value) | Value == "" | Value == "0" ~ 0,
      str_detect(Value, "M") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Value, "[€M★-]")) * 1000000,
      str_detect(Value, "K") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Value, "[€K★-]")) * 1000,
      str_detect(Value, "^€?[0-9.]+$") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Value, "€")),
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    wage_numeric = case_when(
      is.na(Wage) | Wage == "" | Wage == "0" ~ 0,
      str_detect(Wage, "K") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Wage, "[€K★-]")) * 1000,
      str_detect(Wage, "^€?[0-9.]+$") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Wage, "€")),
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    release_clause_numeric = case_when(
      is.na(Release.Clause) | Release.Clause == "" | Release.Clause == "0" ~ 0,
      str_detect(Release.Clause, "M") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Release.Clause, "[€M★-]")) * 1000000,
      str_detect(Release.Clause, "K") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Release.Clause, "[€K★-]")) * 1000,
      str_detect(Release.Clause, "^€?[0-9.]+$") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Release.Clause, "€")),
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )
```

```{r info-section, child = system.file("child_documents/info_section.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```

## Introduction
###

This tutorial covers key concepts from [Chapter 16: Factors](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/factors.html), [Chapter 17: Dates and Times](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/datetimes.html), [Chapter 18: Missing Values](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/missing-values.html), [Chapter 19: Joins](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/joins.html), and [Chapter 22: Arrow](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/arrow.html) from [*R for Data Science (2e)*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund.

You will learn about working with parquet files using **[arrow](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/r/)**, manipulating categorical variables with **[forcats](https://forcats.tidyverse.org/)**, handling date/time data with **[lubridate](https://lubridate.tidyverse.org/)**, managing missing values, and performing joins. Key concepts include opening large datasets from disk without loading them fully into memory, reordering factor levels for plotting, parsing datetime strings, combining datasets with `left_join()`, and dropping rows with missing values.

We recommend using an in-environment AI agent such as [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) or [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code); instructions are written with these tools in mind. You may also use a chat-based AI, but you will need to copy/paste code and data context manually.

### Exercise 1

Create a new repo using `codespace-starter` as a template and name it `r4ds-4`.

If you haven't already, open the `r4ds-4` repo on GitHub and create a Codespace from it.

You need two R Terminals: one for running this tutorial and one for your exercises. Open a second R Terminal using the dropdown button.

Create a new file and save it as `analysis.qmd`. Add a YAML header at the top with a title (`"NBA and FIFA"`) and your name as author. In a bash terminal, run:

```
quarto render analysis.qmd
```

Open `analysis.html` in a browser by right-clicking it in the File Explorer and selecting **Open with Live Server**. Keep this tab open — it will refresh automatically whenever you re-render.

Create a `.gitignore` file with `analysis_files` on the first line and then a blank line. Save and push.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file(".gitignore")
```

If that fails, it is probably because you have not yet loaded `library(tutorial.helpers)` in the R Terminal.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

Parquet files are "column-oriented" — organized column-by-column like R's data frame — which leads to better performance for data analysis than row-oriented CSV files. They also support efficient compression, making them much smaller than equivalent CSV files.

### Exercise 2

In your QMD, put the following in a new code chunk to load all required libraries:

```
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(tidyverse)
  library(arrow)
  library(plotly)
  library(viridis)
  library(scales)
})
```

Also add the following to the YAML header to remove all code echoes from the HTML:

```
execute:
  echo: false
```

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 6)
```

###

Like CSV, parquet is used for rectangular data, but it's a custom binary format designed for big data — not a text format you can read in any editor. Both **arrow** and **dbplyr** provide **dplyr** backends, so you can use the same **dplyr** code whether your data is in a parquet file or a database.

### Exercise 3

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Confirm that the Live Server tab auto-refreshes.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

`open_dataset()` scans a few thousand rows to figure out the structure of the dataset without loading the whole file into memory. You can then use **dplyr** verbs to filter and select before calling `collect()` to bring only the data you need into R.

### Exercise 4

From the R Terminal, run these three commands:

`getwd()`
`dir.create("data")`
`list.files()`

This will create a `data` directory in your project.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look something like this, although your path will be different.

```
> getwd()
[1] "/workspaces/r4ds-4"
> dir.create("data")
> list.files()
 [1] "analysis_files" "analysis.html"  "analysis.qmd"   "data"
>
```

Parquet relies on [efficient encodings](https://parquet.apache.org/docs/file-format/data-pages/encodings/) to keep file size down and supports file compression. This helps make parquet files fast because there's less data to move from disk to memory.


## NBA
###

The [NBA Database](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/wyattowalsh/basketball) is a Kaggle dataset containing 64,000+ games, 4,800+ players, and all 30 teams. Our parquet files `data/game.parquet` and `data/line_score.parquet` contain game and scoring data converted from CSV to parquet format.

Key packages you'll use: **arrow** for reading parquet files, **lubridate** for datetime parsing, **dplyr** for joins and transformations, and **forcats** for ordered factor levels.

```{r nba-data}
open_dataset("data/game.parquet")
```

### Exercise 1

We begin by downloading our parquet files directly from GitHub using `download.file()`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
download.file(
  "https://github.com/PPBDS/vscode.tutorials/raw/refs/heads/main/inst/tutorials/r4ds-4/data/game.parquet",
  destfile = "data/game.parquet",
  mode = "wb"
)
download.file(
  "https://github.com/PPBDS/vscode.tutorials/raw/refs/heads/main/inst/tutorials/r4ds-4/data/line_score.parquet",
  destfile = "data/line_score.parquet",
  mode = "wb"
)
```

CP/CR.

```{r nba-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

`open_dataset()` opens a parquet file and returns an Arrow dataset — it does not load the data into R's memory. Call `collect()` only when you're ready to bring the processed result into R.

### Exercise 2

Ask AI to open `data/game.parquet` using `open_dataset()` and `collect()` to read the full dataset. Don't assign the result to a variable. Add this code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

CP/CR.

```{r nba-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

```{r nba-2-test}
open_dataset("data/game.parquet") |>
  collect()
```

`left_join()` is used to add additional metadata to a dataset — the output always has the same rows as `x` (the left table). Set `relationship = "many-to-many"` when a key can match multiple rows on both sides.

### Exercise 3

Use the following prompt with your AI to build a cleaned and summarized NBA dataset. Add the resulting code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

Here's the prompt we used:

Write R code that: reads `data/game.parquet` using `open_dataset()` and `collect()`; performs a `left_join()` with `open_dataset("data/line_score.parquet") |> collect()` by `game_id` with `relationship = "many-to-many"`; parses `game_date` and `game_date_est` as datetimes using `ymd_hms()`; drops rows where `pts_home.x` is NA using `drop_na()`; converts `game_date` to a datetime with `as_datetime()`, extracts `game_year` using `year()`, and creates `season_label` by combining `season_id %/% 10000` and `str_sub(season_id + 1, 3, 4)` with `paste0()`; filters to rows where `game_date` is not NA and `game_year >= 1946`; converts `wl_home` to a factor with levels "W" and "L"; creates `game_era` using `case_when()` where years ≤ 1979 are "Pre-Three Point", ≤ 1999 are "Modern Era", ≤ 2019 are "Analytics Era", and the rest are "Current Era", then converts `game_era` to a factor with those same levels in order; replaces NA with 0 for all columns containing "pct" and for `pts_home.x` and `pts_away.x` using `across()` and `replace_na()`; adds `total_points = pts_home.x + pts_away.x`; filters to rows where `total_points > 0` and neither home nor away points are NA; groups by `game_year`, `game_era`, `season_label`; summarizes to compute `avg_points` (mean of `total_points`), `games_count` (n()), and `home_win_pct` (mean of `wl_home == "W"`), with `.groups = "drop"`; and arranges by `game_year`.

```{r nba-3}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 6)
```

###

Our result:

```{r nba-3-test}
nba_clean
```

If your result doesn't match ours, replace your chunk code with the following before moving on:

<pre><code>```{r}
open_dataset("data/game.parquet") |>
  collect() |>
  left_join(
    open_dataset("data/line_score.parquet") |> collect(),
    by = "game_id",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    game_date = ymd_hms(game_date),
    game_date_est = ymd_hms(game_date_est)
  ) |>
  drop_na(pts_home.x) |>
  mutate(
    game_date = as_datetime(game_date),
    game_year = year(game_date),
    season_label = paste0(season_id %/% 10000, "-", str_sub(season_id + 1, 3, 4))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(game_date), game_year >= 1946) |>
  mutate(
    wl_home = factor(wl_home, levels = c("W", "L")),
    game_era = case_when(
      game_year <= 1979 ~ "Pre-Three Point",
      game_year <= 1999 ~ "Modern Era",
      game_year <= 2019 ~ "Analytics Era",
      TRUE ~ "Current Era"
    ),
    game_era = factor(game_era, levels = c("Pre-Three Point", "Modern Era", "Analytics Era", "Current Era"))
  ) |>
  mutate(
    across(contains("pct"), ~replace_na(.x, 0)),
    across(c(pts_home.x, pts_away.x), ~replace_na(.x, 0)),
    total_points = pts_home.x + pts_away.x
  ) |>
  filter(total_points > 0, !is.na(pts_home.x), !is.na(pts_away.x)) |>
  group_by(game_year, game_era, season_label) |>
  summarise(
    avg_points = mean(total_points, na.rm = TRUE),
    games_count = n(),
    home_win_pct = mean(wl_home == "W", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(game_year)
```</code></pre>

**lubridate** provides parsing functions named for the order of components: `ymd_hms()` parses "year-month-day hour:minute:second" strings. `case_when()` is inspired by SQL's `CASE WHEN` statement and creates categorical variables from conditions. `factor()` with explicit `levels` preserves the logical ordering even when data is summarized or plotted.

### Exercise 4

Within the latest code chunk, add the option: `#| cache: true`. Assign the result of the pipe to `nba_clean`. If you have not done so already, add `analysis_cache` to your `.gitignore`.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. This first render will take some time — by including `#| cache: true`, Quarto saves the result to disk so the next render loads from cache instead of re-running. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r nba-4}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 8)
```

###

`across()` applies a function to multiple columns selected by name, position, or predicate. `replace_na()` replaces NA values with a specified default — useful when NAs represent zero counts rather than truly missing data. `drop_na()` removes rows where any of the specified columns are NA.

### Exercise 5

Ask AI to pipe `nba_clean` into a line plot showing average points per game over time, with color mapped to `game_era`. Add a point layer sized by `games_count`. Within `labs()`, add a proper title, subtitle, and caption. Don't assign the plot. Add this code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r nba-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 12)
```

###

```{r nba-5-test}
nba_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(x = game_year, y = avg_points, color = game_era)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(size = games_count), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 6), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "NBA Scoring Evolution: 75+ Years of Basketball",
    subtitle = "Average points per game by season with era classifications",
    x = "Season Year",
    y = "Average Points Per Game",
    color = "NBA Era"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, color = "gray60"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

`scale_color_viridis_d()` applies the viridis color scale to discrete variables — it's perceptually uniform and accessible to colorblind viewers. You can wrap a static `ggplot` in `ggplotly()` from the **plotly** package to add interactivity with hover tooltips.

## FIFA
###

The [FIFA 21 dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/yagunnersya/fifa-21-messy-raw-dataset-for-cleaning-exploring/data) is scraped from [Sofifa.com](https://sofifa.com) and contains raw information about thousands of players including ratings, positions, value, and club. Our parquet file `data/fifa.parquet` contains this data.

Key packages you'll use: **stringr** for text cleaning, **lubridate** for date parsing, **dplyr** for conditional transformations, and **forcats** for factor manipulation.

```{r fifa-data}
"data/fifa.parquet" |>
  open_dataset() |>
  collect() |>
  glimpse()
```

### Exercise 1

We begin by downloading our parquet file `data/fifa.parquet` directly from GitHub using `download.file()`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
download.file(
  "https://github.com/PPBDS/vscode.tutorials/raw/refs/heads/main/inst/tutorials/r4ds-4/data/fifa.parquet",
  destfile = "data/fifa.parquet",
  mode = "wb"
)
```

CP/CR.

```{r fifa-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

The FIFA dataset is "messy" — heights and weights include units as text, financial values use "M" and "K" suffixes, club names contain newline characters, and dates use a non-standard format. Real data often requires this kind of cleaning before analysis.

### Exercise 2

Ask AI to open `data/fifa.parquet` using `open_dataset()` and `collect()`, then use `glimpse()` to examine the structure. Don't assign the result. Add this code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

CP/CR.

```{r fifa-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

```{r fifa-2-test}
"data/fifa.parquet" |>
  open_dataset() |>
  collect() |>
  glimpse()
```

`mdy()` from **lubridate** parses dates in "month-day-year" format (e.g., "Jun 17, 2018"). `str_extract()` from **stringr** pulls the first substring matching a pattern — `"\\d+"` matches one or more digits, extracting the numeric part from strings like "185cm" or "76kg".

### Exercise 3

Use the following prompt with your AI to build a cleaned FIFA player dataset. Add the resulting code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

Here's the prompt we used:

Write R code that: opens `data/fifa.parquet` using `open_dataset()` and `collect()`; creates `height_cm` and `weight_kg` by extracting numeric digits from `Height` and `Weight` using `str_extract(.x, "\\d+")` and converting to numeric with `as.numeric()`; removes newline characters (`"\\n"`) from all character columns using `mutate(across(where(is.character), ~str_replace_all(.x, "\\n", "")))`; trims whitespace from all character columns using `str_trim()`; parses `Joined` as a month-day-year date with `mdy()` and assigns it to `joined_date`; calculates `years_at_club` as `as.numeric(today() - joined_date) / 365.25`; creates `long_tenure` as TRUE if `years_at_club > 10`; drops rows where `joined_date` is NA; and creates `value_numeric`, `wage_numeric`, and `release_clause_numeric` using `case_when()` to handle: missing/empty/zero values (→ 0), strings containing "M" (strip "€M★-" characters and multiply by 1,000,000), strings containing "K" (strip "€K★-" and multiply by 1,000), plain numeric strings starting with optional "€" and digits (strip "€" and convert), and everything else (→ 0).

```{r fifa-3}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 6)
```

###

Our result:

```{r fifa-3-test}
final_fifa
```

If your result doesn't match ours, replace your chunk code with the following before moving on:

<pre><code>```{r}
"data/fifa.parquet" |>
  open_dataset() |>
  collect() |>
  mutate(
    height_cm = as.numeric(str_extract(Height, "\\d+")),
    weight_kg = as.numeric(str_extract(Weight, "\\d+"))
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~str_replace_all(.x, "\\n", ""))) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~str_trim(.x))) |>
  mutate(
    joined_date = mdy(Joined),
    years_at_club = as.numeric(today() - joined_date) / 365.25,
    long_tenure = years_at_club > 10
  ) |>
  drop_na(joined_date) |>
  mutate(
    value_numeric = case_when(
      is.na(Value) | Value == "" | Value == "0" ~ 0,
      str_detect(Value, "M") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Value, "[€M★-]")) * 1000000,
      str_detect(Value, "K") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Value, "[€K★-]")) * 1000,
      str_detect(Value, "^€?[0-9.]+$") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Value, "€")),
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    wage_numeric = case_when(
      is.na(Wage) | Wage == "" | Wage == "0" ~ 0,
      str_detect(Wage, "K") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Wage, "[€K★-]")) * 1000,
      str_detect(Wage, "^€?[0-9.]+$") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Wage, "€")),
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    release_clause_numeric = case_when(
      is.na(Release.Clause) | Release.Clause == "" | Release.Clause == "0" ~ 0,
      str_detect(Release.Clause, "M") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Release.Clause, "[€M★-]")) * 1000000,
      str_detect(Release.Clause, "K") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Release.Clause, "[€K★-]")) * 1000,
      str_detect(Release.Clause, "^€?[0-9.]+$") ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(Release.Clause, "€")),
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )
```</code></pre>

`across()` applies a transformation to many columns at once — `where(is.character)` selects all character columns, while `contains("pct")` selects columns whose names include "pct". `str_remove_all()` removes all occurrences of a pattern; combined with `case_when()`, it handles the various currency string formats found in real data.

### Exercise 4

Within the latest code chunk, add the option: `#| cache: true`. Assign the result of the pipe to `final_fifa`. If you have not done so already, add `analysis_cache` to your `.gitignore`.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. This first render will take some time. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r fifa-4}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 8)
```

###

`fct_reorder()` reorders a factor's levels by the values of another variable — use it before plotting to make bar charts and dot plots display in a meaningful order rather than alphabetically. `fct_lump()` collapses rare factor levels into an "Other" category, keeping the top `n` most common levels.

### Exercise 5

Ask AI to use `final_fifa` to create a scatter plot of `Age` vs `X.OVA` (overall rating) with point size and color mapped to `value_numeric`. Filter to players with `value_numeric > 0` and `X.OVA > 60`. Use a viridis color scale and proper labels in `labs()`. Don't assign the plot. Add this code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r fifa-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 12)
```

###

```{r fifa-5-test}
final_fifa |>
  filter(value_numeric > 0, X.OVA > 60) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Age, y = X.OVA, size = value_numeric, color = value_numeric)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(0.5, 8),
                        labels = label_currency(prefix = "€", suffix = "M", scale = 1e-6),
                        name = "Market Value") +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma",
                        labels = label_currency(prefix = "€", suffix = "M", scale = 1e-6),
                        name = "Market Value") +
  labs(
    title = "FIFA Player Performance vs Age",
    subtitle = "Size and color represent market value",
    x = "Age",
    y = "Overall Rating",
    caption = "Data: FIFA 21 dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

Ordered factors (created with `ordered()` or `factor()` with explicit `levels`) tell **ggplot2** to use the specified order for legends and axes, rather than sorting alphabetically. `label_currency()` from the **scales** package formats numbers as currency strings with prefixes, suffixes, and scaling.

## Summary
###

This tutorial covered key concepts from [Chapter 16: Factors](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/factors.html), [Chapter 17: Dates and Times](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/datetimes.html), [Chapter 18: Missing Values](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/missing-values.html), [Chapter 19: Joins](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/joins.html), and [Chapter 22: Arrow](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/arrow.html) from [*R for Data Science (2e)*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund.

You learned about working with parquet files using **[arrow](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/r/)**, manipulating categorical variables with **[forcats](https://forcats.tidyverse.org/)**, handling date/time data with **[lubridate](https://lubridate.tidyverse.org/)**, managing missing values, and performing joins.

### Exercise 1

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd` to ensure that everything works. Check your Live Server tab — the resulting HTML page should be attractive, showing clean versions of your plots.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd")
```

CP/CR.

```{r summary-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 30)
```

###

`arrow` only understands some R expressions, so you may not be able to write exactly the same code you usually would before calling `collect()`. Find a complete list of currently supported functions in `?acero_compute`.

### Exercise 2

Publish your rendered QMD to GitHub Pages. In a bash terminal, run:

```
quarto publish gh-pages analysis.qmd
```

If the bash terminal is still running from rendering, stop it first using `Ctrl/Cmd + C`.

Copy/paste the resulting URL below.

```{r summary-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 1)
```

###

Publishing the QMD creates a public URL anyone can use to view the rendered project.

### Exercise 3

Commit and push all your files. Copy/paste the URL to your GitHub repo.

```{r summary-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

GitHub repositories are where people can view the code you used to create your project.

```{r download-answers, child = system.file("child_documents/download_answers.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```
